stem: During thermal processing of spent NMC lithium-ion batteries at 700 °C in an oxidative atmosphere for 90 minutes, carbon content is reduced from approximately 41% to under 1%. Separately, fluorine-containing electrolyte salt decomposes at temperatures that depend on whether organic solvents are present: decomposition can begin near 85 °C with solvents present, versus 150–250 °C without. After thermal treatment, fluorine is found not only in the gas phase as HF but also retained in an organic oil by-product. An engineer proposes that extending the treatment time beyond 90 minutes at 700 °C will further reduce residual fluorine in the solid product. Which of the following assessments are consistent with the evidence?
options: A. Carbon removal at 700 °C is effectively complete within the first 30 minutes, so extending treatment time beyond 60 minutes provides no meaningful additional carbon reduction.
B. Fluorine retention in the organic oil fraction means that gas-phase HF measurement alone would underestimate total fluorine release during thermal treatment.
C. The engineer's proposal to extend treatment time to reduce solid-phase fluorine is well-supported, because longer oxidative exposure will volatilize the remaining fluorine-containing oil fraction.
D. The early onset of LiPF6 decomposition in solvent-containing systems near 85 °C implies that fluorine release begins well before the carbon oxidation regime is reached at processing temperatures.
E. Because sample weight stabilizes within 60–90 minutes at 700 °C, the thermal treatment protocol is confirmed to have also eliminated all fluorine-containing residues from the solid product.
F. The presence of fluorine in the polar fraction of the oil by-product indicates that fluorine partitioning between gas and condensed phases is a relevant consideration for waste stream management.

stem: A lithium-ion battery pack containing LFP cells is configured without electrical tab connections between cells. During a fault, one cell undergoes complete electrochemical energy release over approximately 10 seconds. A separate simulation then forces the same cell into thermal runaway by applying an instantaneous 250°C temperature rise under adiabatic boundary conditions. An engineer must judge which conclusions about pack-level thermal runaway propagation (TRP) are admissible across these two scenarios.
options: A. Complete electrochemical energy release from a single LFP cell is insufficient to raise the abused cell to the decomposition onset temperature, so TRP does not occur in the electrically isolated pack.

B. The instantaneous 250°C forcing under adiabatic conditions causes TRP because the adiabatic boundary prevents heat dissipation, not because the LFP chemistry itself is inherently prone to propagation.

C. Under adiabatic conditions with the 250°C forcing, adjacent cells decompose partially due to heat transfer from the abused cell, confirming that proximity alone determines TRP risk regardless of boundary condition.

D. Switching from adiabatic to convective boundary conditions with the same 250°C forcing reduces adjacent cell decomposition and can prevent full TRP, demonstrating that heat dissipation rate is a controlling factor in propagation.
E. Because TRP does not occur under electrochemical energy release alone, LFP packs are safe against TRP under all external thermal forcing scenarios.
F. The two scenarios are mechanistically equivalent because both ultimately deposit thermal energy into the abused cell.

stem: During thermal runaway propagation tests on an 18650-type battery module, two parameters are measured: the temperature at which thermal runaway onset occurs in each cell (T2,p) and the rate of temperature rise at that onset moment (q(T2,p)). The T2,p values across propagation events deviate within ±10% of the single-cell accelerating rate calorimetry reference value, while q(T2,p) deviates by over 900% across the same events. Separately, the maximum heat accumulation rate during propagation is measured at approximately 20 times the maximum net heat transfer rate achievable in the module. An engineer proposes using q(T2,p) as the primary diagnostic parameter for characterizing propagation severity across different module designs. Which conclusions about this proposal and the underlying thermal dynamics are admissible?
options: A. The proposal is inadmissible because q(T2,p) exhibits variability far exceeding the range that would allow reliable cross-module comparison.
B. T2,p is a more reliable characterization parameter than q(T2,p) for propagation events because its deviation from the reference value remains within an acceptable range.

C. The large ratio of heat accumulation rate to net heat transfer rate implies that increasing the heat transfer capacity of the module by a factor of two would be sufficient to suppress single-cell thermal runaway.

D. The difficulty in suppressing single-cell thermal runaway, compared to suppressing propagation, is consistent with the observed disparity between heat accumulation rate and maximum achievable heat transfer rate.
E. Because T2,p remains stable across propagation events, it can be used to infer the internal temperature distribution of each cell at the moment of onset.
F. The 900% deviation in q(T2,p) indicates that the heat generation mechanism changes fundamentally between propagation events, requiring a different electrochemical model for each event.

stem: A CTP battery module undergoes thermal runaway in a closed nitrogen-filled enclosure instrumented with pressure and voltage sensors. Engineers observe that abrupt chamber pressure changes occur slightly before the recorded thermal runaway trigger times for each cell in the module. The vented gas composition is dominated by H2, CO2, and CO, with a calculated explosion limit substantially wider than methane and a lower explosive limit near 6%. Which conclusions about monitoring strategy and gas hazard are admissible given these conditions?
options: A. Pressure monitoring in the enclosed space provides earlier warning of thermal runaway onset than voltage monitoring alone.
B. The wide explosion limit range and low explosive limit of the vented gas indicate that the gas mixture poses a higher explosion risk upon air ingress than a pure methane leak of equivalent volume.
C. Because the enclosure is filled with nitrogen, the vented gas cannot form a flammable mixture and the explosion limit calculation is irrelevant to operational safety.
D. The dominance of H2 in the vented gas composition is consistent with conditions of advanced thermal runaway or deflagration rather than initial venting stages where CO2 typically dominates.
E. The higher TRP temperature and lower mass loss observed in the closed nitrogen environment compared to open air indicate that nitrogen confinement reduces the severity of thermal runaway.

F. Since pressure change precedes voltage-based TR detection by only a small margin, voltage monitoring remains sufficient as the primary safety signal and pressure monitoring adds no operationally meaningful lead time.

stem: A cylindrical lithium-ion cell undergoes accelerating rate calorimetry. Internal and surface thermocouples are both active. The internal thermocouple records a maximum temperature of approximately 995°C and a peak rate of temperature rise near 530°C/s. The surface thermocouple simultaneously records a maximum of approximately 566°C and a peak rate near 49°C/s. Separately, the cell voltage drops sharply from above 3 V to below 0.3 V within one second, and this voltage event is recorded roughly 7 seconds before the internal thermocouple indicates the onset of the most intense thermal phase. Which conclusions are admissible given these observations?
options: A. Surface temperature measurements alone are sufficient to characterize the severity of the internal thermal event during thermal runaway.
B. The sharp voltage drop can serve as an early warning signal because it precedes the most intense thermal phase by several seconds.

C. The order-of-magnitude difference between internal and surface temperature rise rates means that surface measurements systematically underestimate the rate of internal heat generation during thermal runaway.
D. Because the voltage drop occurs before the peak internal temperature, it must be caused by separator melting producing an internal short circuit.
E. The internal temperature peak being nearly 430°C above the surface peak implies that surface-based thermal models will overestimate the time available for intervention.
F. The voltage dive preceding thermal runaway onset indicates a diagnostic window exists, but the surface temperature at that moment does not yet reflect the internal state.

stem: A thermal runaway model for a lithium-ion cell is calibrated using oven test data. The model includes SEI decomposition, anode-electrolyte reactions, and cathode decomposition reactions, but excludes combustion of flammable gases. The model accurately predicts the temperature at which thermal runaway initiates but consistently underestimates the maximum temperature reached. A separate model from the literature that omits several exothermic reactions also predicts onset correctly but fails on maximum temperature. Which statements about these modeling results are admissible?
options: A. Combustion of flammable gases is the primary reason the model underestimates maximum temperature, because combustion is the largest exothermic event in thermal runaway.
B. Accurate prediction of thermal runaway onset does not require inclusion of all exothermic reactions, but accurate prediction of maximum temperature does.
C. The exclusion of combustion from the model is justified on the basis that combustion energy primarily heats the surroundings rather than the cell itself.
D. Because the model predicts onset correctly, it can be used to determine the maximum temperature with acceptable accuracy for safety-critical design decisions.
E. The consistent underestimation of maximum temperature across models with incomplete reaction sets suggests that omitted exothermic reactions contribute meaningfully to peak temperature.
F. Including combustion in the model would necessarily resolve the maximum temperature underestimation.

stem: A lithium-ion battery pack is subjected to thermal abuse inside a confined aircraft cabin. Batteries are positioned at a corner adjacent to a wall. Natural ventilation is the only active airflow mechanism. As the number of simultaneously venting cells increases from a single cell to several dozen cells, an engineer must assess which conclusions about gas hazard are admissible. Which of the following statements are supported by engineering reasoning under these conditions?
options: A. The hazardous flammable gas volume scales nonlinearly with cell count, and the duration of hazardous conditions also increases, compounding risk beyond what single-cell extrapolation would predict.
B. Corner and wall-adjacent placement suppresses gas accumulation by directing venting gases away from occupied zones, reducing peak concentration relative to centrally located packs.

C. Buoyancy-driven stratification causes high-concentration gas to persist near the ceiling rather than dispersing uniformly, creating a localized hazard zone that surface-level sensors may not detect.

D. Under natural ventilation, increasing the number of venting cells from one to several dozen causes only a proportional, linear increase in hazardous volume, so single-cell test data is sufficient for multi-cell risk assessment.
E. The corner placement of the pack tends to promote localized gas accumulation rather than suppress it, which is mechanistically consistent with reduced mixing from boundary walls.

F. Because buoyancy effects are dominant, forced ventilation at any flow rate will fully eliminate ceiling-level stratification and reduce peak concentration proportionally with increasing flow speed.

stem: A lithium-ion battery is subjected to thermal runaway under hydraulic oil immersion. At 25 mm and 45 mm immersion heights, no thermal runaway occurs (peak temperature rise rates below 1°C/s), but the fractional effective dose (FED) for gas asphyxiation remains above 1. At 75 mm full immersion, FED drops below 1 and hydrocarbon gas generation is eliminated. Separately, as SOC increases from 0% to 100% under immersion, peak surface temperature decreases while HF concentration increases. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Preventing thermal runaway through partial immersion is not sufficient to eliminate serious asphyxiation hazard, because FED can remain above 1 even when temperature rise rates are well below the thermal runaway threshold.
B. At higher SOC under immersion, the reduction in peak surface temperature indicates reduced electrochemical energy release, which also reduces HF generation as a correlated byproduct.

C. Full immersion eliminates the flammability hazard from hydrocarbon gases and reduces asphyxiation risk below the critical FED threshold, but these benefits are achieved simultaneously only at the 75 mm depth.

D. The increase in HF concentration with increasing SOC under immersion conditions suggests that the mechanism driving HF generation is distinct from the mechanism driving peak temperature, since the two trends move in opposite directions.
E. Because partial immersion suppresses thermal runaway, it also guarantees that all toxic gas hazards are reduced to safe levels, making full immersion unnecessary from a safety standpoint.

F. The non-monotonic relationship between SOC and hazard profile under immersion means that optimizing immersion depth for temperature suppression alone may not minimize the overall chemical hazard to personnel.

stem: In a confined battery enclosure, three cell configurations are tested: closely nested (no gap), 2 mm air gap, and 2 mm FR-PP thermal barrier. The FR-PP material melts between 148–160°C, begins decomposing at 280°C, and forms an insulating char up to 500°C. The air gap configuration shows radiation-dominated heat transfer with convective contributions only during venting. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. The FR-PP barrier prevents propagation partly because its phase-change absorption and subsequent char formation provide two sequential thermal resistance mechanisms rather than a single one.
B. In the air gap configuration, eliminating the venting/breaching phase would not significantly alter the dominant heat transfer mode, since radiation governs throughout the process.

C. The closely nested configuration produces higher peak enclosure pressure and temperature than the air gap configuration, indicating that direct cell contact accelerates both thermal and mechanical energy release.
D. The FR-PP barrier's effectiveness is primarily attributable to its convective blocking capability during venting, since radiation is already the dominant mode in the air gap scenario.
E. Because radiation dominates in the air gap scenario, replacing the air gap with a radiation-absorbing solid material would necessarily outperform the FR-PP barrier in all propagation metrics.
F. The 2 mm air gap delays propagation relative to closely nested cells because it eliminates conductive heat transfer between cells in direct contact.
G. The FR-PP barrier can be considered effective only if enclosure peak temperatures remain below its decomposition onset at 280°C, since char formation is not a designed protective function.

stem: A parallel-connected 18650 NCM battery pack is subjected to a high-current overcharge event at approximately 3C rate. The pack includes cells both with and without current interrupt devices (CIDs). Engineers observe that CID-equipped cells interrupt current but several still progress to combustion, while the cell lacking a CID reaches thermal runaway significantly faster. Which conclusions about the role of CIDs under this condition are admissible?
options: A. The CID provides a meaningful delay to thermal runaway onset under high-rate overcharge, even though it cannot prevent the eventual escalation of internal side reactions.
B. The CID's current interruption function is sufficient to prevent thermal runaway at high charging rates, provided it activates before the battery voltage reaches the overcharge ceiling.
C. The absence of a CID eliminates the only available current interruption mechanism, causing earlier thermal runaway onset compared to CID-equipped cells under the same high-rate overcharge.
D. At high overcharge rates, the rate of internal heat and gas accumulation can outpace the protective response of the CID, rendering it unable to fully suppress thermal runaway.
E. Because CID-equipped cells still experience combustion at 3C, the CID provides no measurable protective benefit under any overcharge condition.
F. The probability of combustion at 3C overcharge rate is high enough that CID presence alone should not be treated as a sufficient safeguard against thermal runaway at that rate.

stem: A lithium-ion battery array undergoes thermal runaway in one cell. Without intervention, all remaining cells reach thermal runaway within 5 seconds. Engineers test two water spray strategies: continuous spray (20 s effective duration) and intermittent spray at two different cycling periods—both at the same duty cycle of 0.5. The 5-second period intermittent spray fails to prevent propagation; the 2-second period spray prevents propagation by holding a neighboring cell surface near 100°C. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The failure of the 5-second period spray despite equal duty cycle to the 2-second period spray demonstrates that total water volume delivered is not the sole determinant of propagation suppression.
B. Continuous spray with 20 s duration is the most effective strategy tested because it delivers the highest total heat dissipation over the suppression window.

C. The 2-second period spray succeeds because its average cooling power during the approximately 5-second propagation interval is sufficient to keep the neighboring cell below its critical temperature.

D. Intermittent spray with a 5-second period fails because its off-phase duration matches or exceeds the propagation interval, allowing the neighboring cell to accumulate sufficient heat between spray pulses.
E. Continuous spray prevents thermal runaway propagation by delivering sustained cooling that eliminates the thermal gradient between cells.
F. Suppression effectiveness depends on cooling power delivered during the critical propagation window, not merely on total energy removed over the full intervention duration.
